diseases.name: Common Cold
diseases.description: A mild viral infection of the upper respiratory tract caused by rhinoviruses. It is highly contagious and spreads through droplets or contact with infected surfaces.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.dosage: 500mg–1000mg every 4–6 hours as needed
medications.usage: Relieves mild fever and pain associated with cold symptoms

medications.name: Cetirizine
medications.dosage: 10mg once daily
medications.usage: Relieves runny nose, sneezing, and nasal congestion

medications.name: Pseudoephedrine
medications.dosage: 60mg every 4–6 hours
medications.usage: Nasal decongestant that reduces nasal congestion and stuffiness

diet.summary: Focus on warm, soothing foods and adequate hydration to support immune recovery and ease symptoms.
foods_to_eat: Warm chicken soup or broth
Honey and ginger tea
Citrus fruits (oranges, lemons) rich in Vitamin C
Garlic and onions for antimicrobial properties
Yogurt with live cultures to support gut immunity
Leafy greens and vegetables rich in antioxidants
Oatmeal for sustained energy
foods_to_avoid: Dairy products that may thicken mucus
Sugary snacks and desserts that suppress immunity
Fried and fatty foods that slow digestion
Alcohol which dehydrates and impairs immunity
diet.hydration: Drink at least 8–10 glasses of water daily. Warm herbal teas, clear broths, and warm lemon water are highly beneficial.

lifestyle.exercise: Avoid strenuous exercise. Light walking indoors is acceptable if you feel up to it. Rest is the priority.
lifestyle.sleep: Aim for 9–10 hours of sleep per night. Elevate your head with an extra pillow to ease nasal congestion.
lifestyle.stress_management: Practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing and meditation to allow your immune system to focus on recovery.
general_tips: Wash hands frequently with soap and water for at least 20 seconds
Use a humidifier to add moisture to indoor air
Avoid close contact with others to prevent spreading the virus
Gargle with warm salt water to soothe a sore throat

diseases.name: Influenza
diseases.description: A contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses that infect the nose, throat, and sometimes the lungs. Influenza is more severe than the common cold.

medications.name: Oseltamivir
medications.dosage: 75mg twice daily for 5 days
medications.usage: Antiviral agent that reduces severity and duration of influenza if started within 48 hours of symptoms

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.dosage: 400mg every 6–8 hours with food
medications.usage: Reduces fever, body aches, and inflammation

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.dosage: 500mg–1000mg every 4–6 hours as needed
medications.usage: Alternative fever and pain relief, gentler on the stomach

diet.summary: High-nutrient, easy-to-digest foods that support immune function and replenish lost fluids.
foods_to_eat: Hot chicken or vegetable soups
Broths with electrolytes
Fruits rich in Vitamin C such as oranges and kiwi
Ginger and turmeric teas with anti-inflammatory properties
Bananas and applesauce that are easy to digest
Whole grain toast for gentle energy
Honey for soothing throat and antimicrobial effects
foods_to_avoid: Alcohol and caffeinated beverages that cause dehydration
Processed and sugary foods
Greasy and heavy meals that tax the digestive system
Raw shellfish or undercooked foods
diet.hydration: Drink 10–12 glasses of fluids daily — water, electrolyte solutions, herbal teas, and clear broths to compensate for fever-related fluid loss.

lifestyle.exercise: Complete bed rest is recommended during acute illness. Gradually resume light activity only after fever has resolved for at least 24 hours.
lifestyle.sleep: Prioritize 9–10 hours of sleep. Good sleep accelerates immune response and recovery.
lifestyle.stress_management: Keep stress minimal. Avoid work obligations during illness. Gentle breathing exercises can aid comfort.
general_tips: Stay home and avoid contact with others to prevent spreading influenza
Cover your mouth when coughing or sneezing with a tissue or elbow
Get an annual flu vaccine to protect against common strains
Monitor for warning signs such as difficulty breathing or persistent high fever

diseases.name: Pneumonia
diseases.description: An infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid or pus. Pneumonia can range from mild to life-threatening and is most serious for infants, older adults, and those with weakened immune systems.

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.dosage: 500mg every 8 hours or 875mg every 12 hours for 5–7 days
medications.usage: First-line antibiotic for community-acquired bacterial pneumonia

medications.name: Azithromycin
medications.dosage: 500mg on day 1, then 250mg once daily for 4 days
medications.usage: Covers atypical organisms; used in combination or for penicillin-allergic patients

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.dosage: 400mg every 6–8 hours with food
medications.usage: Manages fever and chest discomfort

diet.summary: Nutrient-dense, anti-inflammatory diet to support lung healing and immune response.
foods_to_eat: Protein-rich foods like eggs, lean chicken, and legumes for tissue repair
Turmeric milk for its anti-inflammatory curcumin content
Foods rich in Vitamin C (citrus, bell peppers) to boost immunity
Whole grains like oatmeal for sustained energy
Probiotic-rich yogurt to restore gut flora during antibiotic use
Leafy greens (spinach, kale) for antioxidants and micronutrients
foods_to_avoid: Alcohol which suppresses the immune system
Tobacco smoke which further damages lung tissue
Sugary foods and sodas that promote inflammation
Cold and icy foods that may irritate respiratory passages
diet.hydration: Drink 8–12 glasses of water per day to thin mucus secretions and ease expectoration. Warm herbal teas and broths are especially beneficial.

lifestyle.exercise: Strict bed rest during acute illness. Deep breathing exercises (incentive spirometry) help expand lungs and aid recovery. Resume activity only after medical clearance.
lifestyle.sleep: Sleep in a semi-upright position (30–45°) to reduce breathing difficulty. Aim for 10+ hours of sleep.
lifestyle.stress_management: Mindfulness and calm breathing reduce anxiety associated with breathlessness. Avoid any physical or emotional stress during recovery.
general_tips: Complete the full antibiotic course even if symptoms improve early
Seek immediate medical care if breathing worsens or oxygen saturation drops
Get pneumococcal and flu vaccines to prevent future episodes
Use steam inhalation to ease congestion and aid mucus clearance

diseases.name: Asthma
diseases.description: A chronic respiratory condition in which the airways narrow, swell, and produce extra mucus, making breathing difficult. Asthma can be triggered by allergens, exercise, air pollution, or respiratory infections.

medications.name: Salbutamol Inhaler
medications.dosage: 1–2 puffs (100mcg each) every 4–6 hours as needed; 2 puffs before exercise
medications.usage: Short-acting bronchodilator (rescue inhaler) for acute relief of bronchospasm

medications.name: Fluticasone
medications.dosage: 100–250mcg inhaled twice daily (maintenance therapy)
medications.usage: Inhaled corticosteroid for long-term prevention and control of asthma symptoms

medications.name: Montelukast
medications.dosage: 10mg once daily in the evening
medications.usage: Leukotriene receptor antagonist for maintenance therapy and exercise-induced asthma prevention

diet.summary: Anti-inflammatory diet that avoids known food triggers and supports lung health.
foods_to_eat: Apples and grapes rich in quercetin, a natural bronchodilator
Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) rich in omega-3 fatty acids to reduce airway inflammation
Ginger tea for its anti-inflammatory and bronchodilatory effects
Turmeric (curcumin) for reducing airway inflammation
Leafy green vegetables for magnesium and antioxidants
Vitamin D-rich foods (fortified milk, eggs) — low Vitamin D is linked to worse asthma control
foods_to_avoid: Sulfite-containing foods (wine, dried fruits, preserved foods) — common asthma triggers
Processed and fast foods high in trans fats
Dairy products if they worsen mucus production for you
Salicylate-rich foods if aspirin-sensitive
diet.hydration: Drink 8 glasses of water daily to keep airways moist and thin mucus secretions.

lifestyle.exercise: Exercise regularly but choose low-trigger activities like swimming, walking, or yoga. Always carry your rescue inhaler. Warm up and cool down gradually.
lifestyle.sleep: Ensure 7–9 hours of sleep. Keep bedrooms free of dust mites and pet dander. Use allergy-proof pillow and mattress covers.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress and strong emotions can trigger asthma attacks. Practice diaphragmatic breathing, yoga, and mindfulness meditation.
general_tips: Identify and avoid personal asthma triggers (allergens, smoke, cold air, strong odors)
Always carry your rescue inhaler and ensure it is not expired
Monitor peak flow readings regularly to assess asthma control
Keep windows closed during high-pollen seasons and use HEPA air filters indoors

diseases.name: COVID-19
diseases.description: A respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. Symptoms range from mild (cold-like) to severe (requiring hospitalization). Some patients experience long-term symptoms (Long COVID).

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.dosage: 500mg–1000mg every 4–6 hours as needed
medications.usage: First-line treatment for fever and body aches in mild to moderate COVID-19

medications.name: Vitamin D3
medications.dosage: 2000–4000 IU once daily
medications.usage: Immune modulation and reduction of COVID-19 severity risk in deficient patients

medications.name: Dexamethasone
medications.dosage: 6mg once daily for 10 days (severe/hospitalized cases only)
medications.usage: Corticosteroid used in severe COVID-19 to reduce cytokine storm and mortality in patients requiring oxygen

diet.summary: Immune-boosting, anti-inflammatory diet with emphasis on micronutrient sufficiency and hydration.
foods_to_eat: Citrus fruits and berries high in Vitamin C
Turmeric and ginger teas for anti-inflammatory benefits
Lean proteins (eggs, chicken, legumes) to support immune cell production
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, nuts, legumes
Fermented foods (yogurt, kefir) for gut microbiome support
Dark leafy greens for micronutrients and antioxidants
Easily digestible foods (rice, soup, toast) during GI symptoms
foods_to_avoid: Alcohol which suppresses the immune system and dehydrates
Highly processed and sugary foods that increase inflammation
Greasy and heavy meals that are difficult to digest
Caffeine in excess which may worsen insomnia during recovery
diet.hydration: Drink at least 8–10 glasses of water or fluids daily. Oral rehydration salts (ORS) if experiencing diarrhea. Coconut water and clear broths are beneficial.

lifestyle.exercise: Complete rest during acute illness. Gentle breathing exercises and pulse oximetry monitoring are important. Gradually resume light walking after recovery.
lifestyle.sleep: Prioritize 9–10 hours of quality sleep. Prone positioning (lying on stomach) can improve oxygenation in mild hypoxia.
lifestyle.stress_management: COVID-19 can cause significant mental health strain. Practice mindfulness, connect virtually with loved ones, and seek psychological support if needed.
general_tips: Isolate from household members to prevent transmission
Monitor oxygen levels using a pulse oximeter — seek care if SpO2 falls below 94%
Get vaccinated and boosted to reduce severity of future infections
Watch for long COVID symptoms and consult a doctor if symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks

diseases.name: Type 2 Diabetes
diseases.description: A chronic metabolic condition characterized by high blood sugar levels resulting from insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. It is strongly linked to obesity, physical inactivity, and genetic predisposition.

medications.name: Metformin
medications.dosage: 500mg twice daily with meals, titrated to 1000mg twice daily
medications.usage: First-line medication that reduces hepatic glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity

medications.name: Glipizide
medications.dosage: 5mg once daily before breakfast, adjustable to 40mg/day
medications.usage: Sulfonylurea that stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells

medications.name: Sitagliptin
medications.dosage: 100mg once daily
medications.usage: DPP-4 inhibitor that enhances incretin effect; well-tolerated with low hypoglycemia risk

diet.summary: Low glycemic index diet to control blood sugar levels, manage weight, and prevent complications.
foods_to_eat: Non-starchy vegetables (broccoli, spinach, cucumber, cauliflower)
Whole grains (brown rice, barley, oats) with low glycemic index
Lean proteins (chicken, fish, tofu, legumes) to maintain satiety
Berries and apples with fiber that slow glucose absorption
Nuts and seeds (almonds, walnuts, flaxseeds) for healthy fats
Greek yogurt (unsweetened) for protein and probiotics
Cinnamon which may improve insulin sensitivity
foods_to_avoid: White bread, white rice, and refined grains with high glycemic index
Sugary beverages including sodas, fruit juices, and energy drinks
Sweets, desserts, and candy
Saturated fats and trans fats from fried and processed foods
High-sodium processed foods that raise blood pressure
diet.hydration: Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily. Avoid sugar-sweetened drinks. Herbal teas and infused water are good alternatives.

lifestyle.exercise: Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week (brisk walking, cycling, swimming). Include 2–3 resistance training sessions weekly. Exercise improves insulin sensitivity significantly.
lifestyle.sleep: Maintain 7–9 hours of quality sleep. Poor sleep worsens insulin resistance and blood sugar control.
lifestyle.stress_management: Chronic stress elevates cortisol, which raises blood sugar. Practice yoga, meditation, and progressive muscle relaxation.
general_tips: Monitor blood glucose levels regularly using a glucometer
Maintain a healthy body weight — even 5–10% weight loss can significantly improve blood sugar control
Never skip meals; eat at regular intervals to prevent blood sugar spikes and crashes
Schedule regular HbA1c tests every 3 months to assess long-term glucose control

diseases.name: Hypertension
diseases.description: A chronic condition where blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated (≥130/80 mmHg). Often asymptomatic, it is a major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney failure.

medications.name: Amlodipine
medications.dosage: 5mg once daily, titrated to 10mg once daily if needed
medications.usage: Calcium channel blocker that relaxes blood vessel walls and reduces blood pressure

medications.name: Lisinopril
medications.dosage: 10mg once daily, titrated to 20–40mg once daily
medications.usage: ACE inhibitor that relaxes blood vessels, reduces blood pressure, and protects kidneys in diabetic patients
contraindications: History of angioedema with ACE inhibitors

medications.name: Hydrochlorothiazide
medications.dosage: 12.5–25mg once daily in the morning
medications.usage: Thiazide diuretic that reduces fluid volume and lowers blood pressure

diet.summary: DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet emphasizing low sodium, high potassium, and heart-healthy foods.
foods_to_eat: Potassium-rich foods: bananas, sweet potatoes, spinach, avocado
Low-fat dairy products rich in calcium
Whole grains (oats, whole wheat bread, brown rice)
Lean meats and fish especially fatty fish with omega-3s
Berries (blueberries, strawberries) for blood pressure-lowering flavonoids
Garlic and beetroot juice with proven BP-lowering effects
Nuts and seeds (unsalted) for magnesium and healthy fats
foods_to_avoid: High-sodium foods: table salt, processed meats, canned soups, pickles
Alcohol which raises blood pressure
Saturated and trans fats from red meat and fried foods
Caffeine in excessive amounts
Sugary beverages and sweets
diet.hydration: Drink 6–8 glasses of water daily. Limit sodium in fluids. Hibiscus tea has been shown to modestly lower blood pressure.

lifestyle.exercise: Aim for 30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling) 5 days per week. Avoid very high-intensity exercise without medical clearance.
lifestyle.sleep: Ensure 7–8 hours of sleep. Sleep apnea is a common cause of treatment-resistant hypertension; get screened if suspected.
lifestyle.stress_management: Chronic stress is a significant driver of high blood pressure. Regular meditation, deep breathing exercises, and yoga can reduce systolic BP by 5–10 mmHg.
general_tips: Monitor blood pressure at home using a validated device and log readings
Reduce sodium intake to less than 2300mg per day (1500mg is ideal)
Maintain a healthy weight — every 1kg of weight loss can reduce systolic BP by ~1 mmHg
Never stop blood pressure medications abruptly without medical advice

diseases.name: Migraine
diseases.description: A neurological condition characterized by intense, debilitating headaches often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. Migraines can last from hours to days.

medications.name: Sumatriptan
medications.dosage: 50–100mg at onset; may repeat 50–100mg after 2 hours if needed
medications.usage: Triptan that selectively constricts dilated cranial blood vessels to abort acute migraine attacks

medications.name: Topiramate
medications.dosage: 25mg once daily at night, titrated to 100mg twice daily over weeks
medications.usage: Preventive therapy to reduce frequency and severity of migraine attacks

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.dosage: 400–600mg at migraine onset
medications.usage: OTC analgesic for mild-to-moderate migraines; most effective when taken early

diet.summary: Identify and eliminate dietary triggers while maintaining regular meal times to prevent migraine episodes.
foods_to_eat: Magnesium-rich foods: spinach, dark chocolate, avocado, nuts (magnesium deficiency linked to migraines)
Omega-3 rich fatty fish like salmon and sardines for their anti-inflammatory effects
Ginger tea for its anti-nausea and pain-modulating properties
Riboflavin (B2)-rich foods: dairy, lean meats, eggs for migraine prevention
Water-rich fruits and vegetables to maintain hydration
Complex carbohydrates to prevent blood sugar drops that trigger migraines
foods_to_avoid: Tyramine-containing foods: aged cheeses, cured meats, fermented foods
Alcohol especially red wine and beer
Caffeine (or excessive caffeine; withdrawal can also trigger attacks)
Processed and artificial sweeteners including aspartame
MSG-containing foods (certain processed snacks, Chinese takeaways)
diet.hydration: Dehydration is a major migraine trigger. Drink at least 8–10 glasses of water daily. Carry a water bottle and drink consistently throughout the day.

lifestyle.exercise: Regular moderate exercise (walking, swimming, yoga) 3–4 times per week reduces migraine frequency. Avoid intense exercise during a migraine attack.
lifestyle.sleep: Maintain a strictly regular sleep schedule — both insufficient and excessive sleep can trigger migraines. Aim for 7–9 hours.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress is the most common migraine trigger. Practice biofeedback, progressive muscle relaxation, cognitive behavioral therapy, and meditation.
general_tips: Keep a migraine diary to identify personal triggers (food, weather, hormonal, stress-related)
Lie in a dark, quiet room at the onset of an attack
Apply cold or warm compresses to the head or neck during attacks
Establish regular daily routines to maintain consistency and reduce triggers

diseases.name: Gastritis
diseases.description: Inflammation of the stomach lining that can be acute or chronic. It is caused by H. pylori infection, NSAIDs, excessive alcohol, or stress, leading to pain, nausea, and digestive discomfort.

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.dosage: 20mg once daily before breakfast for 4–8 weeks
medications.usage: Proton pump inhibitor that dramatically reduces stomach acid production, allowing healing

medications.name: Ranitidine
medications.dosage: 20mg twice daily or 40mg once daily at bedtime
medications.usage: H2 receptor antagonist that reduces stomach acid secretion

medications.name: Sucralfate
medications.dosage: 1g four times daily on an empty stomach
medications.usage: Forms a protective coating over the inflamed stomach mucosa, promoting healing
side_effects: May reduce absorption of other medications
contraindications: Should be separated from other medications by 2 hours

diet.summary: Gentle, easily digestible, low-acid diet that soothes the stomach lining and reduces acid irritation.
foods_to_eat: Plain cooked rice and boiled potatoes — easy on the stomach
Banana and melon — gentle fruits that coat the stomach
Oatmeal with no added sugar — soothing and easy to digest
Lean chicken or fish steamed without spices
Probiotic-rich yogurt (plain) to restore gut bacteria and combat H. pylori
Honey — shown to have antimicrobial properties against H. pylori
Cabbage juice — traditionally used to heal gastric ulcers
foods_to_avoid: Spicy foods, hot peppers, and chili that irritate the stomach lining
Alcohol which damages the stomach mucosa
Coffee, tea, and caffeinated drinks that stimulate excess acid production
Acidic foods: citrus fruits, tomatoes, vinegar-based dressings
NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen) that damage the stomach lining
diet.hydration: Drink 6–8 glasses of water throughout the day — not with meals (to avoid diluting digestive enzymes). Chamomile and licorice root teas are soothing.

lifestyle.exercise: Light exercise such as walking is beneficial. Avoid exercise immediately after meals. Intense exercise may worsen symptoms during flares.
lifestyle.sleep: Avoid eating 2–3 hours before bedtime. Elevate the head of your bed 6–8 inches to reduce acid reflux during sleep.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress increases stomach acid production. Practice daily stress reduction: yoga, progressive muscle relaxation, and avoiding anxiety-provoking situations.
general_tips: Eat small, frequent meals (5–6 times daily) rather than 3 large meals
Chew food thoroughly and eat slowly to ease digestion
Avoid lying down immediately after eating
Test for and treat H. pylori infection if confirmed (requires antibiotics)

diseases.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
diseases.description: An infection affecting any part of the urinary system (kidneys, bladder, or urethra). Most commonly caused by E. coli. Women are significantly more susceptible than men.

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.dosage: 250–500mg twice daily for 3–7 days (uncomplicated UTI)
medications.usage: Broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic effective against common UTI pathogens

medications.name: Nitrofurantoin
medications.dosage: 100mg (modified-release) twice daily for 5 days OR 50mg four times daily for 7 days
medications.usage: Preferred antibiotic for uncomplicated lower UTI due to high urinary concentration and low resistance rates

medications.name: Trimethoprim
medications.dosage: 200mg twice daily or 400mg once daily for 7 days
medications.usage: Alternative antibiotic for uncomplicated UTI; check local resistance patterns

diet.summary: High-fluid diet with foods that support urinary tract health and inhibit bacterial adhesion.
foods_to_eat: Cranberries and cranberry juice (unsweetened) — proanthocyanidins prevent E. coli from adhering to bladder walls
Blueberries with similar antiadhesion properties as cranberries
Probiotic-rich yogurt to restore healthy vaginal flora
Garlic with natural antimicrobial and immunostimulatory properties
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, bell peppers) that acidify urine and inhibit bacterial growth
High-fiber vegetables and whole grains to prevent constipation that can worsen UTI
foods_to_avoid: Alcohol which irritates the bladder
Caffeine (coffee, tea, sodas) which increases urgency and irritation
Spicy foods that irritate the bladder mucosa
Sugary foods and drinks that promote bacterial growth
diet.hydration: Drink at least 8–10 glasses of water daily to flush bacteria from the urinary tract. Adequate hydration is the single most important dietary intervention for UTI prevention.

lifestyle.exercise: Light to moderate exercise is fine. Ensure you urinate before and after exercise. Avoid tight, non-breathable workout clothes.
lifestyle.sleep: Rest adequately. Incomplete bladder emptying at night can predispose to UTIs; urinate before bed.
lifestyle.stress_management: Chronic stress weakens immune defenses, making UTIs more likely. Practice stress-reduction techniques.
general_tips: Urinate shortly after sexual intercourse to flush out bacteria
Wipe from front to back after using the toilet to prevent E. coli spread
Wear breathable cotton underwear and avoid tight-fitting clothing
Complete the full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve early to prevent recurrence and resistance

diseases.name: Anxiety Disorder
diseases.description: A group of mental health conditions characterized by excessive worry, fear, and apprehension that interfere with daily functioning. Includes generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), panic disorder, and social anxiety disorder.

medications.name: Escitalopram
medications.dosage: 10mg once daily in the morning; may increase to 20mg after 1 week
medications.usage: First-line SSRI for generalized anxiety disorder; takes 2–4 weeks for full therapeutic effect

medications.name: Alprazolam
medications.dosage: 0.25–0.5mg three times daily (short-term use only, up to 4 weeks)
medications.usage: Fast-acting benzodiazepine for acute anxiety relief; intended for short-term only due to dependence risk

medications.name: Buspirone
medications.dosage: 7.5mg twice daily, titrated to 15–30mg per day
medications.usage: Non-benzodiazepine anxiolytic; no sedation or dependence; takes 2–4 weeks for effect

diet.summary: Nutrient-dense diet focused on serotonin precursors, magnesium, and omega-3s to support brain health and reduce anxiety.
foods_to_eat: Complex carbohydrates (oats, quinoa, sweet potatoes) that promote serotonin production
Tryptophan-rich foods (turkey, eggs, cheese, nuts) that are precursors to serotonin
Magnesium-rich foods (dark chocolate, almonds, spinach, avocado) that calm the nervous system
Omega-3 fatty acids (salmon, walnuts, flaxseeds) that reduce neuroinflammation
Probiotic foods (yogurt, kefir, kimchi) supporting the gut-brain axis
Chamomile and green tea for mild calming effects
foods_to_avoid: Caffeine which increases cortisol and worsens anxiety symptoms
Alcohol — a depressant that worsens anxiety long-term despite short-term relief
High-sugar foods causing blood sugar spikes and crashes that trigger anxiety
Processed and fast foods that disrupt gut-brain axis
Energy drinks high in caffeine and stimulants
diet.hydration: Stay well-hydrated with 8 glasses of water daily. Even mild dehydration can worsen anxiety and mood. Herbal teas (chamomile, lavender, passionflower) are calming alternatives.

lifestyle.exercise: Regular aerobic exercise (30 minutes, 5 days/week) is one of the most effective natural treatments for anxiety. It reduces cortisol and increases endorphins and BDNF.
lifestyle.sleep: Sleep hygiene is critical — maintain regular sleep and wake times, keep bedroom cool and dark, avoid screens before bed. Anxiety and insomnia are bidirectional.
lifestyle.stress_management: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is the gold standard psychological treatment. Practice mindfulness meditation, progressive muscle relaxation, and diaphragmatic breathing daily.
general_tips: Practice the 4-7-8 breathing technique (inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec) during acute anxiety
Limit social media and news consumption which amplifies anxiety
Maintain regular social connections with supportive people
Journaling daily can help process anxious thoughts and identify triggers

diseases.name: Depression
diseases.description: A major mood disorder causing persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest or pleasure in activities. It affects thinking, feeling, behavior, and physical health. It is one of the leading causes of disability worldwide.

medications.name: Sertraline
medications.dosage: 50mg once daily; may be titrated to 200mg/day
medications.usage: First-line SSRI antidepressant with broad efficacy and favorable side effect profile

medications.name: Fluoxetine
medications.dosage: 20mg once daily in the morning; may increase to 60mg/day
medications.usage: SSRI antidepressant with longest half-life, reducing withdrawal risk; also approved for OCD, bulimia, panic disorder

medications.name: Mirtazapine
medications.dosage: 15mg once daily at bedtime; titrated to 45mg/day
medications.usage: NaSSA antidepressant; especially useful in depression with insomnia and poor appetite due to sedating and appetite-stimulating effects

diet.summary: Mediterranean-style diet that promotes neurotransmitter balance, reduces inflammation, and supports overall brain health.
foods_to_eat: Fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) rich in omega-3 DHA/EPA that support brain health
Dark leafy greens (spinach, kale) for folate, which is critical for serotonin synthesis
Berries and colorful fruits high in antioxidants that reduce neuroinflammation
Nuts and seeds (walnuts, flaxseeds) for omega-3s and magnesium
Fermented foods (kefir, yogurt, sauerkraut) for gut-brain axis support
Dark chocolate (70%+ cocoa) for flavanols that improve mood
Eggs for vitamin D, B12, and choline essential for neurotransmitter production
foods_to_avoid: Alcohol which is a depressant and worsens depression long-term
Highly processed and sugary foods that cause blood sugar crashes and inflammation
Refined carbohydrates (white bread, pastries) with inflammatory effects
Artificial additives and trans fats that disrupt gut microbiome
diet.hydration: Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily. Dehydration negatively impacts mood and energy. Avoid excessive caffeine which disturbs sleep, worsening depression.

lifestyle.exercise: Exercise is a powerful antidepressant — 30–60 minutes of moderate exercise 3–5 times per week increases serotonin, dopamine, and BDNF. Walking in nature is especially beneficial.
lifestyle.sleep: Depression profoundly disrupts sleep. Practice good sleep hygiene. Consistent sleep-wake schedules, limiting screen time before bed, and cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) are effective.
lifestyle.stress_management: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is highly effective for depression. Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) prevents relapse. Social engagement and meaningful activities are protective.
general_tips: Seek professional help from a psychiatrist or psychologist — depression is a medical condition requiring treatment
Maintain social connections and avoid isolation even when motivation is low
Set small, achievable daily goals to build momentum and a sense of accomplishment
Engage in activities that previously brought pleasure — behavioral activation is a core therapeutic strategy

diseases.name: Hypothyroidism
diseases.description: A condition in which the thyroid gland produces insufficient thyroid hormone, slowing the body's metabolism. Autoimmune Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common cause in developed countries.

medications.name: Levothyroxine
medications.dosage: 25–50mcg once daily in the morning on an empty stomach, titrated based on TSH levels (typically 50–200mcg/day)
medications.usage: Synthetic T4 thyroid hormone replacement — the primary and most effective treatment for hypothyroidism

medications.name: Selenium Supplement
medications.dosage: 200mcg once daily
medications.usage: Selenium is essential for thyroid hormone metabolism; supplementation may reduce thyroid antibodies in Hashimoto's thyroiditis
side_effects: Hair loss at very high doses

medications.name: Vitamin D Supplement
medications.dosage: 1000–2000 IU daily (higher doses if severely deficient — follow lab-guided dosing)
medications.usage: Vitamin D deficiency is extremely common in hypothyroidism and autoimmune thyroid disease; supplementation supports immune regulation and may reduce antibody levels

diet.summary: Thyroid-supportive diet rich in iodine, selenium, and zinc, while avoiding goitrogens in excess.
foods_to_eat: Iodine-rich foods (seaweed, iodized salt, seafood, dairy) essential for thyroid hormone synthesis
Selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts — 1–2 per day, tuna, eggs, sunflower seeds)
Zinc-rich foods (oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas) supporting thyroid function
Lean proteins for muscle maintenance and metabolism
Fruits and vegetables for antioxidants that protect thyroid tissue
Whole grains for fiber (hypothyroidism causes constipation)
foods_to_avoid: Excess raw goitrogenic vegetables (broccoli, cabbage, cauliflower, kale) — cooking reduces goitrogens significantly
Soy products in large amounts which can interfere with levothyroxine absorption
Gluten (for Hashimoto's patients — emerging evidence supports gluten-free diet)
High-fiber foods within 4 hours of taking levothyroxine as they reduce absorption
diet.hydration: Drink 8 glasses of water daily. Hypothyroidism reduces kidney filtration — adequate hydration supports metabolic processes.

lifestyle.exercise: Regular exercise combats hypothyroid fatigue, supports weight management, and improves mood. Start with low-intensity activities (walking, yoga) and gradually increase. Strength training supports metabolism.
lifestyle.sleep: Hypothyroidism often causes fatigue and excessive sleep. Maintain consistent sleep schedules. 7–9 hours is ideal; avoid over-sleeping which worsens fatigue.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress worsens autoimmune thyroid disease. Adequate cortisol management through mindfulness, yoga, and relaxation is important. Cortisol competes with thyroid hormones.
general_tips: Take levothyroxine consistently — same time each day, 30–60 minutes before breakfast or 3–4 hours after evening meal
Do not take levothyroxine with calcium supplements, iron, antacids, or coffee as they impair absorption
Monitor thyroid function (TSH, T3, T4) every 6–12 months or as advised by your doctor
Inform your doctor of all other medications as many interact with levothyroxine

diseases.name: Anemia
diseases.description: A condition characterized by insufficient healthy red blood cells or hemoglobin to carry adequate oxygen to body tissues. Iron-deficiency anemia is the most common type globally, followed by B12 and folate deficiency anemias.

medications.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medications.dosage: 325mg (65mg elemental iron) once or twice daily on an empty stomach (with vitamin C enhances absorption)
medications.usage: Iron supplementation for iron-deficiency anemia — the most common first-line treatment

medications.name: Folic Acid
medications.dosage: 5mg once daily for 4 months (treatment); 400mcg daily (prevention)
medications.usage: Corrects folate-deficiency anemia and is essential for RBC production and DNA synthesis
side_effects: May mask B12 deficiency if used alone in pernicious anemia
contraindications: Vitamin B12 deficiency must be excluded first (folic acid can mask B12 deficiency neurological symptoms)

medications.name: Vitamin B12
medications.dosage: 1000mcg intramuscular injection every other day for 2 weeks, then monthly (pernicious anemia); 1000mcg oral daily (dietary deficiency)
medications.usage: Essential for red blood cell formation, neurological function, and DNA synthesis; corrects B12-deficiency anemia

diet.summary: Iron and vitamin-rich diet that optimizes red blood cell production and maximizes dietary iron absorption.
foods_to_eat: Heme iron sources: red meat, organ meats (liver), poultry, and fish — highest bioavailability
Non-heme iron sources: lentils, beans, chickpeas, spinach, fortified cereals
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, strawberries, kiwi) taken alongside iron-rich meals to enhance absorption
Vitamin B12 sources: meat, fish, dairy, eggs (and fortified foods for vegans)
Folate sources: leafy greens, legumes, fortified cereals, asparagus
Blackstrap molasses — rich in iron and other minerals
foods_to_avoid: Coffee and tea with meals — tannins and polyphenols significantly reduce iron absorption
Calcium-rich foods (dairy) at the same time as iron-rich foods — calcium inhibits iron absorption
Phytate-rich foods (raw bran, unsoaked legumes) without pairing with vitamin C
Alcohol which damages the gut lining and impairs nutrient absorption
diet.hydration: Drink 8 glasses of water daily. Iron supplements can cause constipation — adequate hydration and fiber intake is important.

lifestyle.exercise: Light to moderate exercise is encouraged as tolerated. Severe anemia may limit exercise capacity — start slowly and progress as hemoglobin improves. Avoid overexertion.
lifestyle.sleep: Ensure 8–9 hours of sleep. Anemia causes significant fatigue — adequate rest is essential for recovery.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress can worsen fatigue. Gentle yoga and meditation conserve energy while reducing stress.
general_tips: Take iron supplements consistently — improvement in symptoms may take 2–4 weeks while full correction of stores takes 3–6 months
Identify and treat the underlying cause of anemia (dietary deficiency, blood loss, malabsorption, chronic disease)
Check for gastrointestinal blood loss (peptic ulcer, colon polyps) in adults with iron deficiency anemia
Cook in cast iron cookware — can modestly increase dietary iron content of food

diseases.name: Arthritis
diseases.description: A group of conditions causing joint inflammation, pain, and stiffness. Osteoarthritis (degenerative) and rheumatoid arthritis (autoimmune) are the most common forms. Arthritis is a leading cause of disability.

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.dosage: 400–800mg three times daily with food
medications.usage: NSAID providing effective pain relief and reduction of joint inflammation

medications.name: Naproxen
medications.dosage: 250–500mg twice daily with food
medications.usage: Longer-acting NSAID providing sustained pain relief and inflammation control for arthritis

medications.name: Methotrexate
medications.dosage: 7.5–25mg once weekly (oral or injectable)
medications.usage: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) that slows the progression of rheumatoid arthritis and reduces joint damage

diet.summary: Anti-inflammatory Mediterranean-style diet to reduce joint inflammation, support cartilage health, and maintain healthy body weight.
foods_to_eat: Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) rich in omega-3s that reduce joint inflammation
Colorful fruits and vegetables high in antioxidants (especially berries, cherries, broccoli, spinach)
Olive oil with anti-inflammatory oleocanthal similar to ibuprofen
Garlic, ginger, and turmeric with proven anti-inflammatory properties
Whole grains and legumes for fiber and B vitamins
Low-fat dairy or fortified alternatives for bone-supporting calcium and Vitamin D
foods_to_avoid: Processed and red meats high in saturated fats that promote inflammation
Sugary beverages and high-fructose corn syrup which worsen inflammation
Refined carbohydrates and white flour products
Excessive alcohol that increases uric acid (especially relevant for gout)
High-salt foods that can cause water retention and joint swelling
diet.hydration: Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily. Proper hydration maintains synovial fluid in joints which acts as a lubricant and shock absorber.

lifestyle.exercise: Regular low-impact exercise is essential — it strengthens muscles around joints, reduces stiffness, and maintains function. Swimming, cycling, yoga, and tai chi are excellent choices. Avoid high-impact activities during flares.
lifestyle.sleep: Quality sleep is critical for managing arthritis pain and fatigue. 7–9 hours is recommended. Use supportive pillows and mattresses. Morning stiffness is common — gentle warm-up stretches upon waking help.
lifestyle.stress_management: Stress amplifies pain perception in arthritis. Practice mindfulness, meditation, and pain management techniques. Cognitive behavioral therapy for chronic pain is highly effective.
general_tips: Apply heat before activity to warm up joints and cold therapy after to reduce swelling
Maintain a healthy weight — each kilogram lost reduces knee joint pressure by 4 kilograms
Use assistive devices (ergonomic tools, joint supports, walking aids) to protect joints
Consider occupational therapy to adapt daily activities and minimize joint stress
